tagged_story_for_tts:
Tiny shattered rocks. Earth’s slowest sculptor.

The word sand comes from Old English sand, meaning "grit" or "loose grains"—fitting, since each grain is a fragment of stone, shell, or coral, worn down by wind, water, or ice over thousands of years. A single grain travels like a hiker lost in a storm: bounced by rivers, tumbled by waves, or blown in dunes that creep across deserts at the speed of a snail’s crawl.

Most sand isn’t the golden beaches you imagine. Sahara dunes glow amber from rounded quartz. Hawaii’s black sands are crushed volcanic glass, sharp as tiny shards. Some grains are star-shaped, forged by fish digestive tracts—parrotfish bite coral, grind it to powder, and excrete white sand, producing 80% of the Maldives’ beaches. One cubic meter of desert sand holds roughly 10 billion grains; stacked end-to-end, they’d stretch from New York to Los Angeles and back twice.

Yet sand is disappearing. Humans extract 50 billion tons yearly—more than fossil fuels—for concrete, glass, and landfill. Rivers, dammed and diverted, no longer replenish beaches. Some islands now import sand by ship, a paradox: stealing earth to replace what was stolen.

Three related subjects are glass without melting, deserts that sing, and how oysters build reefs.
